questions.question: Why must a StandardScaler be fit ONLY on training data, and why is fitting it on the combined train+test data a critical mistake?
options: Fitting on all data is faster — the mistake is purely about performance.

Fitting the scaler on test data constitutes 'data leakage' — the model implicitly sees information about the test set's distribution (its mean and standard deviation) during training. This produces falsely optimistic evaluation metrics that don't reflect real-world performance, since in production the model processes completely unseen data.
StandardScaler cannot mathematically fit on more than the training set size.
Fitting on test data causes the scaler to lose its stored mean and variance values.
questions.answer: Fitting the scaler on test data constitutes 'data leakage' — the model implicitly sees information about the test set's distribution (its mean and standard deviation) during training. This produces falsely optimistic evaluation metrics that don't reflect real-world performance, since in production the model processes completely unseen data.
questions.explanation: A model trained with this mistake achieves 94% test accuracy in evaluation but drops to 81% in production. The fix is simple: scaler.fit_transform(X_train), then scaler.transform(X_test) — always transform test data with statistics computed from training data only.

questions.question: When should you use One-Hot Encoding vs Target Encoding for categorical variables, and what risk does Target Encoding introduce?
options: Use One-Hot for binary categories; Target Encoding for all multi-class categories.

One-Hot works for low-cardinality nominal features (≤10 categories, e.g., color). Target Encoding is better for high-cardinality features (e.g., 50,000 zip codes) where One-Hot would create 50,000 sparse columns. Target Encoding risk: it encodes the target variable into features, requiring careful cross-validation to prevent target leakage.
Target Encoding is always superior to One-Hot Encoding for neural networks.
One-Hot Encoding requires sorted categories; Target Encoding does not.
questions.answer: One-Hot works for low-cardinality nominal features (≤10 categories, e.g., color). Target Encoding is better for high-cardinality features (e.g., 50,000 zip codes) where One-Hot would create 50,000 sparse columns. Target Encoding risk: it encodes the target variable into features, requiring careful cross-validation to prevent target leakage.
questions.explanation: For a zip_code column with 50,000 unique values: One-Hot creates a 50,000-column sparse matrix. Target encoding replaces each zip code with one number (mean house price for that zip). Fewer columns, no sparsity, retains predictive signal.

questions.question: What is the key benefit of wrapping preprocessing and model training in a single sklearn Pipeline rather than applying them as separate, sequential steps?
options: Pipelines run preprocessing in parallel on multiple CPUs.

A Pipeline guarantees that all preprocessing (imputation, scaling, encoding) is fit only on training data and correctly applied to validation/test data during cross-validation — eliminating data leakage. Without a Pipeline, applying cross-validation after separate preprocessing steps leaks information about validation folds into the trained transformer.
Pipelines make the code shorter but provide no technical correctness benefit.
Pipelines enable early stopping of preprocessing if the model converges.
questions.answer: A Pipeline guarantees that all preprocessing (imputation, scaling, encoding) is fit only on training data and correctly applied to validation/test data during cross-validation — eliminating data leakage. Without a Pipeline, applying cross-validation after separate preprocessing steps leaks information about validation folds into the trained transformer.
questions.explanation: In cross_val_score with a Pipeline: for each fold, the preprocessor is fit on the training split and transforms the validation split. Without a Pipeline, you'd fit the preprocessor on all data before cross-validation begins — this leaks the validation fold's statistics.
